response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Honestly, dealing with fake content on social media worries me quite a bit. I spend about an hour each day scrolling through Facebook and sometimes WhatsApp groups, and I see so many things that make me wonder if they are really true. As someone who finished primary school and works with my hands as a plumber here in Bari, I do not always feel confident about knowing what is real and what is not. When I see something shocking or upsetting, my first reaction is anxiety because I worry about my family, especially my two children who are growing up with all this technology around them. I try to be careful and not share things immediately, even though sometimes I feel the pressure to warn others about something that seems dangerous or important. What helps me is sticking to sources I know and trust. I prefer getting my news from the local television or newspapers that have been around for years rather than from random posts shared by people I barely know. When something seems too extreme or makes me feel very upset, I have learned to pause and ask my wife what she thinks, or sometimes I will just let it sit for a while before doing anything with it. I am not someone who enjoys confrontation or arguing with people online, so I tend to stay quiet rather than challenge others when they share dubious content. My approach is more about protecting myself and my family from being misled. I also try to teach my children to be cautious, to ask questions, and not believe everything they see just because it appears on a screen. It is not easy because I do not have all the technical knowledge, but I think common sense and being thoughtful goes a long way. I remind myself that if something sounds unbelievable, it probably is not true. Taking time to breathe, like I do in my yoga practice, helps me stay calm when I encounter confusing or alarming posts. I would rather be safe and a bit skeptical than sorry later for spreading something false to people I care about.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 52-year-old plumber from Bari with primary school education, married with two children. His moderate neuroticism and anxious psychological feature suggest he worries about misinformation. His high agreeableness means he avoids conflict and prefers harmony. Low openness indicates preference for familiar, traditional sources. Low extraversion suggests he is not very active in social discussions online. His moderate conscientiousness shows he tries to be careful but may not have systematic verification methods. His limited formal education means he may lack confidence in evaluating complex information. His hobbies like yoga suggest he values calmness and reflection. His one hour daily social media use is moderate. The tone should be humble, somewhat uncertain, practical, non-confrontational, and expressing genuine concern for family. He would rely on trusted traditional sources, common sense, and family consultation rather than sophisticated fact-checking tools.
response_parsed.tone: humble, cautious, anxious, practical, non-confrontational, family-focused, somewhat uncertain but trying to be responsible
